Maladie à Virus Ebola — Situation Report

Democratic Republic of Congo

Published

26 May 2026

Introduction

The Democratic Republic of Congo is experiencing an outbreak of Maladie à Virus Ebola (MVE) with cases reported across health zones in Ituri and Nord-Kivu provinces. This report summarises the epidemiological situation based on data extracted from official INSP DRC situation reports. It is generated automatically and reflects the most recent available data.

Methods

Data sources

Epidemiological data were obtained from PDF situation reports published by the Institut National de Santé Publique (INSP) DRC during the 2026 MVE outbreak in Ituri Province and neighbouring zones. The automated fetcher (fetch_sitreps.py) scrapes two INSP web pages (https://insp.cd/ebola/ and https://insp.cd/category/sitrep/) for PDF hyperlinks and downloads any document whose URL contains at least one of the tokens MVE, SitRep, Ebola, or Marburg and whose WordPress upload path indicates a date of May 2026 or later. A JSON manifest records each downloaded file with its source URL, file size, and download timestamp, ensuring idempotent incremental runs. Eleven PDFs were processed in total, all obtained directly from the INSP DRC website. MD5 verification confirmed that the PDFs downloaded from the INSP website are byte-for-byte identical to those archived in the Kraemer lab repository, establishing that both pipelines operate on the same source documents.

For cross-source validation, pre-harmonised long-format case-count data were obtained from the same Kraemer lab repository (build/long/), which represents independent manual human extraction of INSP DRC situation reports into a standardised schema.

Automated PDF extraction pipeline

Each PDF was passed to the Anthropic Claude claude-sonnet-4-6 large language model via the Messages API as a base64-encoded document, enabling visual (pixel-level) reading of the PDF rather than relying on text-layer extraction. A temperature of 0 was used throughout to maximise response reproducibility.

The extraction pipeline makes two sequential API calls per document. The first call uses a focused structured-extraction prompt (up to 8,192 output tokens) and returns a single JSON object with two keys: new_cases (the table of incident cases for the most recent reporting day, nouvelles données or equivalent) and cumulative (the cumulative summary table, labelled Tableau 3 or données cumulatives in most documents). Each table object carries table-level metadata — the exact table title string, a reporting date, a period end date, a period start date, and any footnote text — as well as an array of row objects. Every row is normalised to eight fields: province, zone_de_sante, cas_suspects, cas_probables, cas_confirmes, deces_suspects, deces_probables, and deces_confirmes. Empty strings are used for fields absent from the source; genuine zero counts are preserved as "0". Where a numeric field is absent from the table itself but is explicitly and unambiguously stated in the document body text or Points Saillants section for the same reporting unit and the same reporting period, the prompt instructs the model to populate that field from the narrative; values are never inferred, estimated, or calculated. The prompt further instructs the model to transpose any pivoted table in which health zones appear as column headers rather than row labels. The second API call (up to 48,000 output tokens) requests a verbatim full-text transcription of the document and is stored in raw_extraction.json alongside the structured data, providing a complete audit trail for each PDF. If the second call fails, the pipeline continues without the transcription rather than aborting.

If the Claude response is not valid JSON, the json_repair library is applied before parsing.

Post-extraction validation

Three successive validation checks guard against spurious data being introduced into the output. First, a title-based check examines the table_title field of the cumulative table object; if the title contains any of a defined set of French alert-investigation keywords (alertes, reçues, validées, investiguées, enquêtées, notifiées, investigation, suivi des alertes) and the extracted rows contain no case-count data, the cumulative rows are discarded and a warning is emitted. This check rejects the Tableau de suivi des alertes format used in some early Ituri DPS provincial documents. Second, a row-level heuristic discards the cumulative table if, in any extracted row, the probable-case count exceeds three times the suspect-case count — a pattern consistent with an alert-investigation table in which the probable column records investigated alerts rather than probable cases. Third, if the cumulative table has been discarded by either check and the new-cases table has no identifiable table title, new-cases rows are also suppressed on the grounds that they were likely synthesised by the model from body text rather than read from a dedicated table.

Date resolution

The end date assigned to cumulative rows follows a defined priority cascade: (1) a date parsed from the cumulative table title using a French-language date pattern (DD mois YYYY); (2) the reporting_date field returned by the model; (3) the period_end_date field; (4) a date parsed from the table footnotes; (5) a date parsed from the PDF filename, which is attempted first as a compact numeric pattern (YYYYMMDD) and then as a French text date (DD-mois-YYYY). For new-cases rows, the filename date is used preferentially as the end date, falling back to the model-reported fields. An empty string is assigned only if no date is recoverable by any method.

Data harmonisation

Zone names are normalised to a canonical spelling prior to analysis: Mungbwalu is corrected to Mongbwalu. Rows are classified as aggregate (province- or outbreak-level summaries) if the health zone field contains the word total or sous-total, is empty while a province name is present, or contains multiple zone names separated by commas or parenthetical zone counts (e.g. Mongbwalu, Rwampara et Bunia (3 ZS)). Sentinel non-data values (ND, N/A, dashes, and em-dashes) are treated as missing.

Suspect and probable case counts are combined into a single unconfirmed category, because individual situation reports differ in which of the two fields they populate: earlier Ituri DPS provincial documents use the probabilités column for zone-level counts, whereas national INSP documents use cas suspects. The unconfirmed count for a row is the sum of suspects and probables, with each coerced to zero when absent but the other is present; when both are absent the unconfirmed value is left as missing rather than set to zero, to prevent false zero observations for zones not reported in a given sitrep. Deaths are derived as the first non-missing value from deaths_suspected, deaths_probable, and deaths_confirmed, in that order of priority. The three-way coalesce is necessary because early Ituri DPS provincial documents record deaths exclusively in the deces_confirmes column, while later national INSP documents use deces_suspects; the column labelling is not consistent across situation reports.

To enforce temporal consistency, cumulative counts are constrained to be monotonically non-decreasing within each health zone using the running cumulative maximum across successive reporting dates. This operation is applied separately to confirmed cases, unconfirmed cases, and deaths. Where multiple source documents provide data for the same health zone and the same reporting date, the row with the highest unconfirmed count is retained.

Output structure

Per-PDF outputs (new cases table, cumulative table, combined linelist, and raw JSON extraction) are written to a dedicated subfolder under outputs/sitreps/. The canonical folder name is derived from the PDF’s entry in a manifest file where available, or from the PDF filename stem otherwise. All processed PDFs are appended to a single master linelist (outputs/master_combined_counts.csv) sorted chronologically by reporting date. A registry (outputs/processed.json) records each processed PDF with its processing timestamp and row count, enabling incremental updates without reprocessing previously analysed documents.

Statistical analysis

All statistical analyses and data visualisations were produced in R (version 4.5.1) using the tidyverse suite of packages [@tidyverse], with figures produced using ggplot2 [@ggplot2] and tables formatted with flextable [@flextable]. The report was rendered with Quarto. This report and the underlying extraction pipeline were developed with the assistance of GitHub Copilot (Microsoft) and the Claude API (Anthropic, model: claude-sonnet-4-6).

Results

As of 24 May 2026, the DRC MVE outbreak had recorded a cumulative total of 1,011 cases (105 confirmed, 906 suspect or probable) and 223 deaths, corresponding to a case fatality ratio of 22.1%. Cases have been reported across 8 health zones in 2 provinces.

Current counts by health zone

Table 1 presents the most recent cumulative case counts disaggregated by health zone, drawn from the latest available situation report for each zone.

Cross-source validation

Cumulative zone-level counts from this pipeline are compared against the Kraemer lab Ebola DRC 2026 repository, which codes INSP DRC situation reports into a standardised long-format by manual human extraction. Both pipelines operate on the same source PDFs (MD5-verified). Zone names differ between sources at two sites (Mongbwalu/Mongbalu and Nyankunde/Nyakunde); counts are matched on the harmonised name. ND values in the Kraemer lab series are treated as missing. Any divergence between the two series therefore reflects differences in extraction method — automated LLM-based visual extraction versus human manual coding — rather than differences in the source documents.

Table 2 presents numerical differences at the most recent date available in each source for each zone.

Response indicators

Discussion

The outbreak trajectory shows a consistent increase in suspect and probable case counts across the reporting period. Confirmed cases remain a minority of the total, reflecting the challenges of laboratory confirmation in the field setting. The geographic spread across both Ituri and Nord-Kivu provinces underscores the need for multi-province coordination of the response.

Per-zone new case data are only available from 20 May 2026 onwards, as earlier situation reports did not disaggregate new cases by health zone. Response indicator data (hospitalisation, contacts traced) are sourced from the Kraemer lab repository as these metrics are not captured in the automated extraction pipeline. Results should be interpreted in the context of reporting delays and the possibility of incomplete situation report coverage for some health zones and dates. The case fatality ratio reported here should be treated as a provisional estimate, as delays in death reporting may affect the numerator, and the deaths column captures the first available of deaths-among-suspects, deaths-among-probables, and deaths-among-confirmed, which are not consistently labelled across situation reports.

MD5 checksum verification confirmed that the PDFs processed by this pipeline are byte-for-byte identical to those archived in the Kraemer lab repository. Divergence in the cross-source comparison therefore reflects either differences in how each extraction method interprets ambiguous table layouts, or outright reading errors in one of the two series.

Four sources of divergence were identified. First, for SitReps N°001 and N°002 (14–15 May), the INSP documents present only an outbreak-wide aggregate without a standardised zone-level cumulative tableau. The N°001 PDF text explicitly states that its 246-case suspect total covers “Zones de Santé (ZS) de Mongbwalu, Rwampara et Bunia” collectively; Kraemer incorrectly attributed this aggregate to Mongbwalu alone. This pipeline extracted the zone-level probable case figures from Tableau 1 in N°002 (57 for Mongbwalu, 27 for Rwampara, 3 for Bunia) and has no zone-specific entry for 14 May, where no zone-level breakdown exists. Second, direct comparison of extracted values against the PDF text reveals reading errors in the Kraemer series for two zone–date combinations: Katwa confirmed cases are recorded as 1 and 0 by Kraemer for 21 and 22 May respectively, whereas both the published PDF text and this pipeline’s extraction agree on 2 and 3; Butembo confirmed cases are recorded as 2 by Kraemer for 21 May, whereas the PDF states 1 and this pipeline extracted 1. A persistent one-case difference in Goma suspected cases (0 in this pipeline versus 1 in Kraemer across 20–23 May) could not be attributed to a specific textual feature and may reflect a cell-border ambiguity. Third, this pipeline enforces monotonic non-decrease via cumulative maximum, masking genuine downward revisions visible in the Kraemer series: Mongbwalu confirmed cases fall from 14 (21 May) to 10 (22 May) in the Kraemer data, most likely reflecting reclassification of four previously confirmed cases; this pipeline retains 14 on 22 May before both series converge at 19 on 23 May. Nyankunde confirmed cases similarly fall from 11 to 9 between 21 and 22 May in the Kraemer data; the cummax retains 11 across 22–23 May. Fourth, three of the PDFs processed here carry explicit draft labels in their filenames (Draft-MVE-Ituri-ActuelB-1, Draft_SitRep_MVE_RDC_20260520_vf1, Draft_SitRep_MVE_RDC_2022vf2_IM); the Kraemer lab may hold the corresponding final published versions, and provisional counts in drafts may differ from figures in the final document. This pipeline has additionally processed SitReps N°009 and N°010 (through 25 May), which extend beyond the current Kraemer series, and several health zones present in the Kraemer series (Aru, Kilo, Nizi, Miti-Murhesa) are excluded from this pipeline’s zone filter.
